steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the text most strongly suggests about Crowley's work. The key tension: Crowley has devoted champions — Bloom read Little, Big 46 times and called it a 'masterwork' — yet he is 'not as well known as the most commercially successful American writers.' The gap between critical devotion and commercial reach is the signal.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Many critics say that it has notable similarities to the work of James Merrill.: The text mentions Merrill only as a champion of Crowley, not as a stylistic comparison. ✗
  • Choice B — The opinion that it includes at least one masterwork is not widely held among literary critics.: The text names specific champions but never says critics broadly disagree; Bloom and Merrill are presented as influential, not fringe. ✗
  • Choice C — Harold Bloom would likely have agreed that it is superior to the work of James Merrill.: No comparison between Crowley and Merrill's writing is made anywhere in the text. ✗
  • Choice D — Some notable figures would agree that it should be more commercially successful than it currently is.: Champions who call a novel a 'masterwork' and read it dozens of times would naturally want wider recognition for it. This is directly supported by the contrast the text draws. ✓
That is the correct answer.
